KOURT KING
Investment case · 2026

The investment
case for padel.

The fastest-growing sport on earth. 35 million players, 150 nations — and no one owns its culture yet.

Before the plan or the numbers — this is the case for the opportunity itself.

The global picture · FIP World Padel Report 2025

A sport exploding on every continent.

35M+
Players worldwide
77,300
Courts globally
150
Nations playing
24,600
Clubs & facilities

In a single year the sport added 14,355 new courts (+15%), with clubs up 16% and federation-registered members up 42%. The FIP now has 100 member national federations — triple the ~30 it had in 2019. This is not a trend levelling off. It's still accelerating.

Source: FIP World Padel Report 2025 (International Padel Federation), presented at the 35th General Assembly, Nov 2025.

The claim

Padel is the fastest-growing
sport in the world.

Not a claim we're making — a claim the data makes for us. Watch how fast this has happened, then look at the numbers underneath it.

▶
Why Padel is Taking Over the World

▶ Watch on YouTube ↗

The trajectory

12 million to 35 million
in a decade.

Nearly 3× the players in ten years — with credible forecasts putting it at 50–70 million by 2030 if courts keep pace. Very few sports in history have moved this fast.

12M
2014
30M
2024
35M+
2025
50–70M
2030 (fcast)

Sources: FIP / World Padel Tour player estimates; industry forecasts (DojoBusiness, 2025).

Why this matters for a media business

The stickiest, broadest audience
in racket sport.

92%

They come back

of players return after their first ever session — an extraordinary retention rate that explains padel's viral pull.

60 / 40

Men to women

one of the most gender-balanced racket sports in the world — a rare, broad, dual-market audience.

25–40

The core

urban professionals at the heart of the game — a premium, monetisable demographic advertisers chase.

A sport people stick with, that draws men and women equally, skewed to a high-value age bracket. For a content and platform business, that's close to the perfect audience — and it's growing every week.

Sources: Playtomic / PwC Strategy& Global Padel Report; FIP 2025.

The two markets that matter to us

Britain's ready. America's next.

United Kingdom
860,000

people played padel in 2025 — more than double the year before, and past one million by 2026. From just 15,000 in 2019.

And the runway is enormous: over 10 million Britons say they want to try it. Courts are racing to keep up — 1,553 by end of 2025, up from 69 in 2020.

Source: LTA participation figures, 2025–26.

United States
+58%

participation growth 2023–25. Courts up from under 100 in 2020 to 450+ in 2025, with 1,200+ courts in the pipeline through 2026.

Forecast 1.4M players by 2028, and 8–15M across North America by 2030 as pipelines unlock. The land grab hasn't happened yet.

Sources: Business Research Insights; MarketReportsWorld 2025.

Validation

Sovereign wealth, blue-chips
and A-list capital are already in.

This isn't an unproven bet on a sport that might take off. The biggest money in the world has already moved — you'd be building on top of validated demand, not creating it.

Sovereign

Qatar Sports Investments acquired the World Padel Tour in 2023 and now owns professional padel through Premier Padel. A nation-state owns the pro game.

Industry

The sport is worth ~€2 billion a year, growing ~15% annually. The padel-club market alone is projected to hit €6 billion by 2026 (Playtomic / Deloitte).

Celebrity

Cristiano Ronaldo streamed Premier Padel to his 73M+ YouTube subscribers. Zlatan Ibrahimović opened his own padel centre. The star power is arriving.

But here's the tell: almost all of that capital sits in the professional tour and the infrastructure — the elite game and the courts. The everyday player, the culture, the content — that layer is still wide open.

Sources: Sportico 2025; Wikipedia / FIP; Playtomic / Deloitte Global Padel Report.

The proof · what happens next

When a participation sport
becomes a spectacle.

The clearest comparison isn't another padel brand — it's Hyrox. A grassroots, mass-participation format that turned everyday athletes into a global event business, now the target of serious private-equity money.

The Hyrox parallel
£40m→£140m

annual revenue in a single leap, as the format scaled.

1.5M

athletes in 2025/26 — up from ~570k the year before.

55–65%

of revenue comes from participation — people paying to take part, not just to watch.

The lesson: the money isn't only in broadcasting the elite — it's in millions of amateurs who want to play the thing they watch.

Padel already has the participation. What it's missing is the show.

The gap

35 million players.
Zero owned culture.

The pros are owned — by Qatar. The courts are being built — 77,300 and counting. The equipment market is mature. But the thing that makes a sport a cultural force — a defining amateur competition, breakout characters, a content brand that owns the conversation — doesn't exist for padel.

Ronaldo streaming a match to 73 million people proves the appetite for padel content is already there. Nobody is feeding it for the everyday player. Every fast-growing sport eventually gets its defining media brand — padel's hasn't been built yet. That is the opening.

Where Kourt King fits

The competition wins the audience.
The content keeps it.

We're not fighting for a slice of equipment sales or building another booking app. We're building the entertainment and culture layer the sport doesn't have — starting with a nationwide hunt for Britain's most watchable amateur, filmed social-first.

01

The show

A creator-era competition that turns unknown amateurs into characters — and every applicant into marketing. Wins the audience fast.

02

The platform

Watch it Sunday, play it Tuesday. Viewers play the same format in mini-leagues, get rated, climb a national leaderboard. The audience becomes a habit.

Do both, and you don't just make content about padel — you become the home of padel content, and eventually part of the sport's infrastructure: the rating, the ranking, the audience.

Where it goes

Britain first.
Built for the world.

The playbook earns its way outward: prove it in Britain, then the US, then license into the mature markets — Spain (17,000 courts), Italy (10,000+), France, the Nordics — and the fast-spending Gulf. Every padel nation is a new edition of the same show and the same platform.

A sport tripling in a decade, backed by sovereign and celebrity capital, with no cultural owner, is the rarest thing in sports media: a global category still up for grabs.

Padel is the opportunity.
Kourt King is the play.

The market is proven, accelerating, and backed by the biggest money in the world. The culture layer is empty. The question isn't whether padel gets its defining media brand — it's who builds it.
